title: Nonstabilizerness Mpemba Effects
authors: Zhenyu Xiao, Hao-Kai Zhang, Shuo Liu
abstract: Quantum state preparation can be strikingly counterintuitive: the fastest route to a target state need not start from the apparently closest initial condition. We uncover such a quantum Mpemba effect in the dynamical generation of quantum magic (nonstabilizerness), quantified by the stabilizer Rényi entropy, in $\mathrm{U(1)}$-symmetric random circuits initialized from tilted product states. States with lower initial magic can generate magic faster than states with higher initial magic. The acceleration is not determined solely by the conserved-charge distribution. Two initial-state families with identical initial magic and identical charge distribution exhibit qualitatively different magic-growth dynamics, depending also on the spatial structure of the initial state within each charge sector. Analogous magic Mpemba effects in $\mathrm{SU(2)}$-symmetric circuits and under nonintegrable Hamiltonian dynamics further show that the phenomenon is tied neither to Abelian symmetry nor to random-circuit dynamics, establishing quantum magic as a distinct arena for Mpemba physics.
summary: The paper identifies a quantum Mpemba effect where the generation of nonstabilizerness (magic) follows counterintuitive trajectories, where less magical states can become more magical than their counterparts over time. This phenomenon is driven by the spatial arrangement of initial charges and is a general feature of symmetric and non-integrable quantum dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating the existence of a quantum Mpemba effect in the dynamical generation of nonstabilizerness (magic), specifically whether states with lower initial magic can overtake states with higher initial magic during evolution.
main_result: The authors discover an inverse Mpemba effect where magic growth trajectories cross, driven by the interplay between charge conservation and the spatial structure of initial states. This effect is shown to be robust across U(1) and SU(2) symmetric random circuits as well as non-integrable Hamiltonian dynamics.
method: The study uses analytical derivations for late-time steady-state magic and numerical simulations of random circuits and the mixed-field Ising model to track early-time dynamics.
model_or_system: The study examines U(1)-symmetric and SU(2)-symmetric brickwork random circuits, as well as the non-integrable mixed-field Ising model, using various tilted product state initial conditions.
key_observables: Second stabilizer Renyi entropy (M2), which quantifies nonstabilizerness or magic.
important_parameters: Tilt angle (theta), system size (N), time (t), and charge-sector distribution p(q).
main_assumptions: The equivalence of certain state families is treated as an asymptotic property of the steady-state limit where intra-sector scrambling is complete.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the circuit structure and the concept of the Mpemba crossing; Figure 2 shows the evolution of magic for different initial states; Supplemental figures show dynamics in SU(2) circuits and the Ising model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quantum Mpemba effect in magic generation, provides analytical derivations for steady-state magic in U(1) circuits, explores the role of spatial structure via TNS and TDWS states, extends the findings to SU(2) and non-integrable Hamiltonian systems, and concludes with a detailed mathematical appendix.
why_interesting: It connects the resource theory of magic with non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, showing how symmetry and spatial configuration can lead to anomalous relaxation phenomena in many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the U(1)-symmetric brickwork ran- dom circuit. Each time step consists of two layers of nearest- neighbor two-qubit gates with periodic boundary conditions. (b),(c) Schematic illustration of magic dynamics without and with an inverse Mpemba effect, respectively.

FIG. 2. Initial and late-time second stabilizer R´enyi entropy densities. (a) Initial value M2(0)/N, which is identical for the families of tilted ferromagnetic states (TFS), tilted N´eel states (TNS), and tilted domain-wall states (TDWS). (b) Late-time global-U(1) Haar prediction for M (∞) 2 /N for TFS (solid lines) and TNS (dashed lines); TDWS coincides with TNS in this limit. Colors indicate system sizes N = 12, 16, 20, 24.

FIG. 3. Magic dynamics under U(1)-symmetric circuit evolution [(a)–(c)] and mixed-field Ising Hamiltonian (MFIM) evolution [(d)] for N = 20 and several tilt angles θ ∈[π/4, π/2]. Panels (a) and (c), corresponding to tilted ferromagnetic states (TFS) and tilted domain-wall states (TDWS), respectively, exhibit inverse-Mpemba crossings, whereas panel (b), corresponding to tilted N´eel states (TNS), shows no inverse Mpemba effect. (d) In contrast to the U(1)-symmetric circuit dynamics in (b), the same TNS initial states under MFIM evolution exhibit Mpemba-type magic dynamics.
